result:
I want to write about the moment the lights come back on after a power outage, because that moment is never just about electricity.  It’s a small resurrection, a hush breaking into applause.  The bulbs flicker once, twice, then hold steady, and every room remembers its name.  You stand in the revived glow holding the exact thought you had when the darkness arrived—sometimes half a sip of wine still in your mouth, sometimes the sentence you were about to speak to someone who is no longer in the house.  The refrigerator shudders awake like a bear disturbed mid-hibernation, and the digital clocks all blink 12:00 in Morse code for “start again.”  You feel time fracture into two eras:  the blacked-out minutes when the world felt honorary and fragile, and the bright aftermath where everything pretends continuity.  But you know the secret:  the atoms have been shuffled; the air has a new serial number.  Nothing is the same as before the darkness, it only agrees to impersonate it.

I want to write about how my mother used to open the windows during thunderstorms, even though the rain lashed sideways onto the sills and the wallpaper bubbled like a slow-motion sigh.  “Let the storm know we’re not afraid of it,” she’d say, as if weather could be disarmed by hospitality.  I thought she was reckless, but now I think she was practicing a kind of emotional judo—inviting the larger force to roll through the house so it wouldn’t have to break the walls.  Years later, when I held her hand in the ICU and the monitors beeped like distant thunder, I opened the window a crack,-rules be damned.  The nurse started to protest, then saw my face and stepped back.  The storm that arrived that night smelled of city concrete and lilacs bloomed two months early.  My mother’s pulse steadied for exactly seven minutes, the way a candle flares before it drowns in its own wax.  I choose to believe the storm accepted the invitation, passed over us, and kept moving, carrying some portion of pain in its clutch of warm wind.

I want to write about the scent of elementary school libraries in the 1980s:  dust, lamination, and the secret vanilla that lives inside yellowed paper.  The card catalogs stood like tiny sarcophagi, each drawer a cemetery of due-date stamps announcing miniature apocalypses.  I liked to pull a card partway out and press my thumb against the black ink of strangers’ names—children I would never meet who once loved the same book enough to borrow it repeatedly.  It felt like holding a séance with fingerprints.  I always hoped the ink would transfer onto me, a benign contagion of curiosity.  Maybe it worked; I still read in the bathtub until the pages swell and the spine sighs apart, until the book becomes a raft I might climb aboard if the bathroom ever floods and I need to sail away from everything I’ve promised to become.

I want to write about the year I tried to grow strawberries on a fire escape seven stories above Brooklyn.  The instructions said “full sun,” but they didn’t specify which hour of the day the sun might be emotionally available.  Mine arrived timidly at 7:13 a.m. and left before the coffee finished dripping.  Still, I coaxed three berries the size of aspirin tablets out of that reluctant dirt.  They blushed fromgreen to a red so sincere it embarrassed the rest of the skyline.  When I finally ate them, standing barefoot on metal grating that still held yesterday’s heat, they tasted like a childhood I never actually had—warm, sweet, and gone in one bite.  I swallowed the seeds without thinking and imagined them journeying through me, looking for a place to anchor.  Some morning soon I’ll cough up a vine and have to decide whether to plant it in a public park or apologize to the mayor for unauthorized foliage.

I want to write about the sound my father’s mechanical pencil made when he balanced the checkbook at 11 p.m. on the last day of every month:  a sharp, rotary click, like a tiny impatient clock.  The sound meant “we are still solvent,” but it also meant “keep your voice low, numbers are fragile.”  I’d pad into the kitchen for water and see him hunched under the hanging lamp, its conical shade creating a spotlight only large enough for two columns of addition.  His handwriting leaned forward as if eager to reach the next month.  I thought those evenings were about money until I had my own mortgage; now I understand they were about choreography—about making the scattered receipts and stubs line-dance into something that resembled a plan.  When he died, I found the pencil in his shirt pocket, lead snapped flush.  I clicked it once out of habit and half expected the estate attorney to appear with a ledger, asking me to justify grief as a line item.

I want to write about laundromats that stay open 24 hours, islands of fluorescence at 3 a.m. where insomniacs fold someone else’s socks while CNN scrolls disasters overhead like ticker-tape parades for anxiety.  I once watched a woman teach her toddler colors by holding up unmatched socks—this one is sea-foam, this one is bruise, this one is the inside of a mouth when the dentist says “wider.”  The child laughed as if colors were reckless jokes the world hadn’t yet apologized for.  Across the room, a man in hospital scrubs slept upright in a plastic chair, his head nodding in quarter-time with a dryer full of hospital blankets that exhaled the smell of chlorine and someone else’s recovery.  I wanted to wake him and say “your shift is over,” but maybe his shift would never be over; maybe he came here nightly to tumble the linens until the stains surrendered, until every ghost of every hallway became warm and softly scented with spring rain detergent and the delusion of starting fresh.

I want to write about the day I realized my neighbor’s rooster is an optimist.  It crows at jet planes, at sirens, at the moon wearing a halo of ice—anything that might, with enough faith, be mistaken for dawn.  I keep thinking someone should sit that bird down and explain astronomy, but perhaps it already knows and chooses delusion the way we choose cardigans or political parties.  Each false sunrise is still a song, still a throat thrown open to possibility.  I record him on my phone some mornings and play it back when the news reports another glacier giving up.  The rooster doesn’t care that I’m cheating; he sings on loop inside my pocket, proclaiming territories that exist only in waveform, coaxing me to crow along until the day brightens or I run out of battery, whichever apocalypse arrives first.

I want to write about how writing itself is a power outage we voluntarily enter.  We shut the breaker to the part of the mind that remembers dinner plans, turn off the grid that measures likelihood, and feel around in the pantry of vocabulary for anything still canned and edible.  Sometimes we open the wrong container and words spill like marbles across the kitchen of the page, gleaming, treacherous.  Readers arrive later, flip the switch, and assume the light was always on.  They see the orderly sentence, the polished metaphor, never hearing the curses we shouted when we stubbed our metaphysical toes on the leg of the table.  That is fine; art is hospitality, and guests deserve the illusion of uninterrupted current.  Still, somewhere in the margins, the darkness lingers—an unused adjective, an erased confession—waiting politely for the next storm, the next invitation to come inside and sit with us until the bulbs decide whether mercy or more light is the braver reply.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
